title: [lichen_l2.c:812-813] net_pkt_read cursor not checked after initialization
description:
Final pass: This was missed because reviewers assumed Zephyr API calls always succeed when parameters are valid.

Root cause: At lines 812-813:
  net_pkt_cursor_init(pkt);
  ret = net_pkt_read(pkt, tx_ipv6_buf, pkt_len);

net_pkt_cursor_init() is declared void in Zephyr (zephyr/net/net_pkt.h) and cannot fail. However, if the packet structure is corrupted (e.g., invalid fragment chain from a bug in the IP stack), net_pkt_read() may fail.

The code correctly checks ret < 0 at line 814-817. But there's an implicit assumption that pkt_len (from net_pkt_get_len at line 792) accurately reflects the available data in the packet.

If net_pkt_get_len returns a value larger than the actual data in the fragment chain (a Zephyr bug or corruption), net_pkt_read would attempt to read past the fragments and fail or read uninitialized memory.

This is a defect in Zephyr's net_pkt abstraction, not this code. But the assumption that pkt_len == actual readable data is not validated.

Impact: Unlikely in practice unless Zephyr's networking stack has bugs. Defense-in-depth would be to trust net_pkt_read's return value and use that as the actual length rather than pkt_len.

Fix (optional): After net_pkt_read, use ret (if >= 0) as the actual read length rather than assuming pkt_len bytes were read.
close_reason: Added NOTE comment explaining net_pkt_cursor_init is void and net_pkt_read error check handles corruption defensively
